title: 2025 05 08 Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-05-08 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Benjamin Plows-Kolff: Fair and affordable food regulations for small food businesses
topic: Petition of Benjamin Plows-Kolff: Fair and affordable food regulations for small food businesses
description: Thursday, 8 May 2025 Petitions Committee Petition of Benjamin Plows-Kolff: Fair and affordable food regulations for small food businesses Benjamin Plows-Kolff, Nicole Hecht & Tracy Johnson Ministry for Primary Industries Vincent Arbuckle, Deputy Director-General, New Zealand Food Safety Jenny Bishop, Director Food Risk Management, New Zealand Food Safety Bruce Arnold, Director Cost Recovery, MPI Maria Gracie, Manager Policy and Performance, Cost Recovery, MPI Ministry for Regulation Paul Delahunty, Deputy Chief Executive, Reviews and System Capability Charlie Hulley, Senior Advisor Petition of Zane Allan: Ban Geoengineering and Weather Modification in New Zealand Zane Allan, Robina Dobbie and Gabrielle Thompson
descriptionSummary: Petition of Benjamin Plows-Kolff: Fair and affordable food regulations for small food businesses. Benjamin Plows-Kolff, Nicole Hecht & Tracy Johnson. Ministry for Primary Industries

We represent small and micro food business owners across who since, uh, the Food Act came into force, have been burdened with extortionately high costs and unnecessarily complicated food safety compliance.

NPIs new Levy is about to make this worse, our concerns have already been brought to government's attention and have been either ignored or dismissed in the pursuit of strong trade agendas To cut to the chase one morning, or, uh, sorry, my one morning a week coffee cart just on Saturdays does not have the same risks involved as a 100 seat restaurant with 10 staff that sells raw oysters.

But my fees and regulatory burden are the same as a large business. Long before I got into this industry, many food businesses were disappearing along with a rich diversity. Kiwis pride themselves on their ability to adapt and thrive purely on their creativity, resilience, and pragmatism, and an ecosystem of unnecessary and excessive regulation. We are stifled from thinking outside the box and doing what we do best to be creative. Problems with the system include audits that can be done from an office with no value other than tick.

Boxing authorities for, uh, sorry. Authorities responsible for deciding whether or not we comply with regulation are also the ones we're supposed to go to for support, information and education. That's like, there's Richmond five minutes away, but I have to do a much bigger registration to trade there.

Cottage industry has very limited early earning potential. Most under about 10,000 a year. They want to be compliant. But when we look at these costs as a percentage of their earnings, it's totally prohibitive and cheaper to not be registered. Our vision for a safe local food industry includes thorough, uh, thorough consultation and engagement with industry, large, small and micro support and, uh, education to help us produce the safest food possible in a practical day to day way. And separating the regulator from the educator and nuanced sliding scale of risk that ensures an appropriate regulatory burden for the type of business.

Uh, prior to the passing of the new Food Act 2014, opponents to the new legislation including concerned members of community and small businesses, have emphatically rallied against it and refreshed opposition continues to arise, although some minor concessions were made, um, to ensure the ACT did not impede on charities holding like a small store or a sausage SLE for fundraising.

Government legislation did not cater for the con cottage industry or small food business. Uh, and the mayor of commented, the community totally supports the need for higher public health safety. Consequences that were clearly articulated to government 15 years ago have been our reality ever since Media releases made, uh, by the government to promote the New Food Act claimed it would reduce compliance costs and regulatory burden.
